150. Permit design needs an __________.

Meaning: systematic evaluation of conditions that expose migrants to abuse

151. Credential recognition supports __________.

Meaning: growth whose opportunities also reach recent arrivals

152. Integration supports __________.

Meaning: development of skills and knowledge

153. __________ changes over the life cycle.

Meaning: net contribution to public finances

154. Migration may support __________.

Meaning: replacement of ageing population

155. Pension reform and migration policy both raise questions of __________.

Meaning: fair sharing of demographic costs and benefits across ages

156. A __________ should reflect actual housing and labour conditions.

Meaning: a locally designed plan connecting services and participation

157. __________ reduces duplication.

Meaning: coordination across agencies

158. __________ avoids stereotypes.

Meaning: policy guided by evidence

159. A __________ treats legal status without erasing personhood.

Meaning: policy that protects dignity, agency and equal treatment

160. __________ involves institutions and migrants.

Meaning: mutual adaptation process

161. New arrivals need time to __________.

Meaning: become established

162. People should not erase identity merely to __________.

Meaning: be accepted socially

163. Stable housing helps families __________.

Meaning: become established in a new place

164. Families may __________ when rents become unaffordable.

Meaning: leave a neighbourhood or area to live elsewhere

165. Migrants may __________ shortage occupations.

Meaning: begin a job or opportunity

166. Some newcomers __________ firms that create local jobs.

Meaning: establish a business or organisation

167. Targeted school support helps pupils __________.

Meaning: reduce a gap in progress or attainment

168. Long waits for classes can make learners __________.

Meaning: make less progress than others

169. Credential recognition can __________ for skilled work.

Meaning: remove an obstacle so that access becomes possible

170. Repeated equal-status contact can __________ stereotypes.

Meaning: gradually weaken a barrier or prejudice

171. Trust can __________ through reliable local institutions.

Meaning: develop gradually and consistently

172. Municipal services should __________ newcomers early.

Meaning: make active contact with a person or group

173. Inspectors must __________ when workers are exploited.

Meaning: intervene when a problem requires a response

174. Migrants may __________ on services because of poor information.

Meaning: lose an opportunity

175. Workers often transfer money home to support relatives.

Meaning: send money to relatives in another country

Integrated original synthesis

4. Original reading: Integration begins after arrival

Read for connections: legal routes, language access, skills recognition, housing, local capacity, demographic change and civic participation.

1 · Movement has different causes and legal routes

Migration is not one movement with one cause. People cross borders for work, education, family, safety and opportunity. Annual migration flows measure movement during a period, while migrant stock describes people living outside their country of birth. Confusing these measures produces exaggerated claims about sudden change.

Most migration is regular and managed through work, study or family routes. Labour migration helps firms fill job vacancies, while family reunification recognises that settlement is rarely an individual economic event. Humanitarian migration and forced displacement involve protection needs that cannot be judged only through labour-market demand.

The first policy challenge is legal clarity. Predictable residence permits, work rights and asylum procedures reduce uncertainty for migrants, employers and public agencies. Irregular migration increases vulnerability because people may avoid healthcare, accept exploitation or depend on smugglers. Expanding regular pathways does not remove the need for border management, but it can reduce dangerous and unregulated movement.

Arrival is only the beginning. Migrant integration concerns participation in work, education, housing, healthcare and civic life. It is often described as a two-way process because institutions also adapt. A school may need language support, an employer may need fair recruitment, and a municipality may need translated information.

The first discipline is definition. International migration includes very different migration flows, while migrant stock measures residents at one point in time. Separating regular pathways from forced displacement prevents one emotional image from becoming an entire policy argument.

Movement data need a complete ledger. Immigrants arrive, emigrants leave and net migration records the difference between them. The interests of an origin country and a host country may overlap, yet work permits, temporary protection and refugee protection serve distinct legal purposes.

2 · Integration connects language, skills and fair work

Language matters, but timing and design matter too. Early language acquisition supports daily independence and employment. Classes should fit work and care schedules and connect with practical situations. Delayed support can make children lose ground and adults miss out on services.

Employment is central because work provides income, contact and recognition. Yet migrants often experience skills underuse. Engineers, nurses and teachers may enter low-skilled jobs because credential recognition is slow or unclear. Better skills matching can clear the way for suitable work and reduce waste of human capital.

Employment alone does not guarantee integration. Newcomers may face an earnings gap, insecure contracts or discrimination. Anti-discrimination law requires inspections and accessible complaints. Recruiters and employers should not exploit a worker’s dependence on one permit or sponsor.

Local institutions carry much of the practical responsibility. One-stop centres can combine information on housing, work, schools and legal status. Agencies should connect with newcomers rather than assume every newcomer understands fragmented administrative systems. Digital services help, but offline support remains necessary.

Early support changes later outcomes. Timely language training, credible credential recognition and careful skills matching reduce skills underuse. Together they improve labour-market inclusion without pretending that employment alone guarantees belonging.

For new arrivals, practical access matters more than a ceremonial welcome. Flexible language classes, reliable healthcare access and well-designed community sponsorship help receiving communities turn first contact into durable civic inclusion.

3 · Housing, schools and everyday contact shape belonging

Housing shapes integration strongly. New arrivals often enter expensive rental housing and may concentrate in districts with limited supply. This can intensify residential segregation and pressure on public services. The cause is not migration alone; long-standing housing shortages and unequal urban development often predate new arrivals.

Schools can prevent or reproduce separation. Diverse classrooms may support language and contact, while school segregation concentrates disadvantage. Teachers need resources, but children should not be treated as permanent outsiders. Outcomes for the second generation reveal whether institutions provide real mobility.

Integration also involves belonging. Intercultural dialogue and ordinary contact can wear away stereotypes, but symbolic festivals cannot replace equal treatment. Civic participation and migrant involvement in policy design improve programmes because affected people identify barriers officials may overlook.

Receiving communities need resources and a voice. Sudden population growth can increase demand for housing, classrooms and healthcare. National governments should finance municipalities according to actual need. Otherwise, residents experience service pressure while central authorities announce the economic benefits of migration from a comfortable distance.

Institutions create the conditions for contact. Integration services and accessible one-stop centres can connect housing, schools and health support. Strong anti-discrimination law, equal treatment and ordinary civic participation then make inclusion visible in everyday decisions.

A persistent employment gap cannot be closed by any job at any wage. Migrant entrepreneurship, faster skills-to-work mobility and wider human-capital development require institutional coordination between employers, regulators and training providers.

4 · Demographic arguments need time, evidence and local capacity

Migration can support ageing societies. Demographic ageing, low birth rates and a rising dependency ratio create demand for workers, particularly in health, construction and care. Migration may slow population decline and expand the tax base, but it cannot permanently reverse ageing by itself because migrants also grow older.

The economic effect changes over time. New arrivals may initially need language and settlement support, while later employment produces taxes and consumption. A serious assessment of fiscal contribution should consider age, employment, family structure and public investment rather than assigning one permanent number to every migrant.

Origin countries also experience gains and losses. Remittances support households and foreign-exchange earnings, while diaspora networks encourage trade and knowledge exchange. However, emigration of doctors or engineers can weaken essential services. Partnerships on training and circular mobility may distribute benefits more fairly.

Public debate often treats migration as either an economic rescue or a social threat. Both simplifications fail. Migration can fill shortages, create businesses and support population renewal, while poorly managed growth can intensify housing and service pressures. These outcomes depend on institutions, timing and local conditions.

Demographic claims also need limits. demographic ageing and a rising dependency ratio may increase demand for workers, yet migration cannot replace family, care and productivity policy. Honest fiscal contribution analysis should therefore track cohorts over time and use clear integration outcome indicators.

Ageing societies may need more care workers, but recruitment is not a complete strategy for population renewal. Demographic fairness requires evidence-based policy and settlement-capacity investment so that demographic gains do not depend on neglected workers or underfunded towns.

5 · Participation turns settlement into shared citizenship

Trust requires honest communication. Governments should publish understandable data, distinguish migration categories and explain uncertainty. Transparent communication does not mean hiding problems; it means avoiding the use of exceptional cases as descriptions of an entire population.

Integration policy should be evaluated through integration outcome indicators: employment quality, language progress, school achievement, housing stability, discrimination and participation. Counting the number of people who attended one course says little about whether they could settle in successfully.

The strongest approach combines control, rights and practical inclusion. It protects borders and asylum standards, recognises qualifications, supports municipalities and involves migrants in decisions. Migration policy becomes sustainable when it treats people neither as temporary labour units nor as permanent strangers, but as participants whose opportunities and responsibilities develop over time.

Belonging is neither automatic assimilation nor permanent difference. Two-way integration asks institutions and newcomers to adapt through intercultural dialogue, inclusive policy dialogue and shared problem-solving. That process strengthens social cohesion when people can participate without having to erase their histories.

Citizenship can formalise belonging, while political participation begins through local voice and association. A dignity-centred approach recognises the integration-policy trade-off between common expectations and unequal starting points, reducing the exclusion spillover costs created by rigid systems.

Continue to model essays

Idea-building model

5. Advanced C2 essay

Question: Does successful integration require newcomers to adapt more than the institutions and communities receiving them?
Extended model · 1236 words · designed to build arguments, not imitate exam length

Ageing societies face a structural problem: fewer workers must support a growing number of retirees while health and care needs increase. Migration appears to offer a direct solution by adding working-age residents. Yet migration is not a demographic machine. Its value depends on employment, integration, housing and public consent.

What migration can change quickly is the size and age profile of the labour force. New workers may fill labour shortages, contribute taxes and support sectors such as care, construction and technology. Countries experiencing population decline may also preserve schools, transport and local markets. However, migration cannot permanently cancel ageing. Migrants grow older, have families and use services. Maintaining one dependency ratio through continuous inflows would require ever-larger numbers. Policy should therefore treat migration as one part of demographic adaptation alongside productivity, retirement reform and family support.

Employment quality is decisive. A qualified worker employed below their skill level contributes less than they could. Slow credential recognition creates skills underuse, lower earnings and frustration. Only when migrants can enter suitable work does demographic gain become economic gain.

Housing creates another constraint. Rapid inflows into cities with limited supply can intensify housing affordability problems. Residents may blame newcomers for shortages created by years of underbuilding. Governments need place-based funding so municipalities can expand schools, transport and rental housing before pressure becomes politically explosive.

Social division is not inevitable, but segregation can reproduce it. Concentrated poverty, weak language support and unequal schools reduce contact and opportunity. Early language training, mixed neighbourhoods and anti-discrimination enforcement can wear away barriers. Symbolic celebration is insufficient when institutions still produce unequal outcomes.

Public consent depends on fairness. Workers may fear wage competition, while migrants may face exploitation. Labour inspection and collective standards protect both groups. Employers should not use migration to avoid improving pay or conditions.

Family settlement can support stability. Family reunification helps people settle in and reduces the emotional costs of separation. Critics view family migration as disconnected from labour demand, but families create care, continuity and long-term participation. The fiscal picture changes over time. Initial education or settlement costs may precede later taxes and entrepreneurship. Public debate has often demanded one immediate verdict, yet migration’s effects have unfolded across decades. Evaluation should therefore use life-cycle and local data.

Origin countries also matter. Recruitment from states already lacking doctors or nurses may solve one care shortage by worsening another. Training partnerships and ethical recruitment can reduce this transfer. Political participation strengthens integration. People affected by rules should contribute to programme design. Local voting rights or consultative bodies can increase responsibility and responsiveness, although citizenship remains the fullest form of membership.

Had governments expanded housing and recognition systems before increasing recruitment, some later conflicts might have been avoided. Capacity must accompany policy ambition. Housing and local capacity determine whether demographic gain becomes social conflict. New workers may be nationally beneficial while particular municipalities face immediate pressure on schools, transport and rental housing. Central government should therefore direct funding towards the places receiving newcomers rather than relying on national averages. Without this place-based investment, residents may experience visible costs long before broader economic benefits appear.

The second generation provides a more demanding test than first-year employment. Children who grow up with stable legal status, mixed schools and access to higher education are more likely to convert migration into long-term social mobility. By contrast, residential and school segregation can preserve disadvantage even when their parents are employed. Integration policy must therefore examine educational progression, earnings and participation across decades rather than celebrating rapid labour-market entry alone.

Public confidence also depends on labour standards. Employers should not be allowed to use migrants as a permanently cheaper or more dependent workforce, because this harms both newcomers and existing workers. Effective inspection, portable permits and access to complaints reduce exploitation while limiting fears of unfair competition. Shared rules at work often support social cohesion more reliably than symbolic cultural campaigns.

Origin countries should not be treated as unlimited reservoirs of trained labour. Recruiting doctors or care workers from systems that already face severe shortages may solve one country's demographic problem by transferring it elsewhere. Ethical recruitment, training partnerships and opportunities for circular mobility can distribute benefits more fairly. Remittances are valuable, but they do not automatically compensate for the loss of essential professionals.

Migration policy needs evidence-based policymaking and honest cost-benefit analysis, both directed towards long-term public value. Equitable access to housing, language support and transport matters to newcomers, established residents and essential workers.

Successful integration develops human capital. Lifelong learning and transferable skills help adults rebuild careers, while targeted support can turn arrival into intergenerational mobility.

Uncertain status and unsafe housing can produce chronic stress and harm mental wellbeing. Secure employment, clean drinking water and fewer structural barriers are therefore integration issues, not peripheral benefits.

Fair decisions recognise individual circumstances and require a clear evidence threshold. Legal safeguards, fewer employment barriers and consistent explanations protect public confidence in migration systems.

Recruitment platforms need algorithmic transparency because automated rankings create information asymmetry. Regulatory oversight and procedural fairness must also protect freedom of expression when applicants challenge discrimination.

Integration databases should follow data minimisation and a legitimate purpose. Independent oversight can close the accountability gap, while municipalities build up the expertise to coordinate records safely.

Newcomers often enter entry-level roles, but technology should enable worker augmentation rather than silent job displacement. Employers can provide paid training and share productivity gains so mobility leads to progression.

Good integration programmes need funding continuity and scientific independence. Mission-driven research, careful replication studies and open knowledge spillovers show which local interventions travel well.

Earth observation and satellite data can help map settlement after disasters. Climate monitoring, weather forecasting and coordinated disaster response also explain why some mobility is an adaptation response rather than a simple choice.

Migration planning increasingly intersects with climate adaptation. Adaptation finance, flood resilience and early-warning systems can reduce forced movement, while managed retreat may still require dignified relocation.

Rural displacement can intensify when biodiversity loss weakens ecosystem services. Protecting soil biodiversity, pursuing nature-positive development and slowing pollinator decline can make livelihoods more secure in places of origin.

Mobility influences food security through farm labour and household income, while market concentration can expose workers to abuse. Fairer supply chains, lower food waste and careful management of water scarcity strengthen both origin and receiving regions.

Rapid population change can expose housing insecurity and sharpen the land-use trade-off. Strong municipal delivery capacity supports sustainable urban development instead of allowing shortages to add to hostility.

A circular economy can create accessible repair and reuse work while reducing the material footprint. Better resource productivity also limits economic externalities and narrows the water-security gap affecting vulnerable districts.

Migrant workers sustain global value-chains and services trade, while trade diversification can create new routes into employment. A shared trade benefit is impossible if mobile workers alone carry the adjustment burden.

Integration depends on community consent and informed resident sentiment, yet neither should become a licence for exclusion. Preventing local displacement, applying place-based policy and pursuing resident-centred growth make belonging a shared local project.

Migration can compensate partly for ageing, but only when newcomers are treated as future participants rather than temporary labour. Not only must policy attract workers, but it must also create conditions in which they and their children can belong and progress. Without that investment, demographic relief may coexist with social division. With it, migration can support renewal while strengthening rather than fragmenting society.

Exam-length model

6. Realistic IELTS essay · approximately 300 words

Question: Some people believe immigrants should adapt completely to the culture of their host country, while others think cultural diversity should be protected. Discuss both views and give your opinion.
Model answer · 312 words

Ageing populations create labour shortages and rising demand for healthcare. Some people therefore regard immigration as necessary, while others argue that governments should develop the existing workforce. In my view, immigration is useful but should complement, not replace, domestic reform.

Immigration can add working-age residents quickly. Migrants may take up vacancies in care, construction and technology, while taxes support public services. What immigration provides is a faster demographic response than changes in birth rates. It can also slow population decline in regions losing young adults. However, relying entirely on migration is unrealistic. Migrants also age, and rapid population growth can increase pressure on housing and schools. Governments should improve productivity, childcare, training and employment among older workers. Only when the existing population is used effectively can migration policy retain social legitimacy.

Integration determines whether migration delivers its potential. Language support and credential recognition help newcomers enter suitable work rather than experience skills underuse. Local authorities need funding for housing and education. Many countries have recruited workers, yet they have invested too little in long-term integration. A balanced policy would admit migrants through transparent routes while enforcing labour standards and supporting municipalities. Domestic workers should not be undercut, and migrants should not be trapped in exploitative jobs.

Had governments addressed labour-force participation earlier, current shortages might be less severe. Implementation must also be local. Municipalities need predictable finance for housing, schools and language support, while employers must offer equal pay and safe conditions. Otherwise immigration may fill national vacancies but intensify visible pressure in particular neighbourhoods. This is precisely how an economically useful policy can lose public legitimacy.

The policy should also be evaluated regionally because national labour shortages do not guarantee that housing and services exist where workers arrive. Better planning can connect recruitment with transport, accommodation and local training. This reduces pressure and makes the economic case more credible to residents.

Why the exam-length essay is strong

Direct position

The introduction treats integration as mutual adaptation while recognising the practical importance of shared civic rules.

Causal explanation

The essay connects language, fair work, housing and everyday contact to independence and belonging.

Developed contrast

Cultural continuity is weighed against the need for communication, legal equality and institutional participation.

Policy mechanism

Accessible classes, credential recognition, anti-discrimination enforcement and local funding turn principle into delivery.

Recycled language

Earlier collocations return as part of the reasoning rather than as decoration.

Controlled complexity

Advanced grammar remains clear enough for realistic exam conditions.

7. Advanced grammar transformations

1. If qualifications were recognised faster, migrants would enter suitable work. (Conditional inversion)

Migrants would enter suitable work were qualifications recognised faster.

2. Governments expanded recruitment before they had built enough housing. (Past perfect)

Governments expanded recruitment before they had built enough housing.

3. Stable legal status matters most for integration. (Cleft sentence)

What matters most for integration is stable legal status.

4. Trust improves only when rules are applied fairly. (Negative inversion)

Only when rules are applied fairly does trust improve.

5. Migration fills vacancies and supports population renewal. (Not only...but also)

Not only does migration fill vacancies, but it also supports population renewal.

6. The programme was designed for inclusion, but it created complex paperwork. (Participle clause)

Designed for inclusion, the programme created complex paperwork.

7. Although language tests are useful, they may exclude vulnerable applicants. (Fronted concession)

Useful though language tests may be, they may exclude vulnerable applicants.

8. Policy should protect rights, support work and fund communities. (Parallelism)

Policy should protect rights, support work and fund communities.

9. Migrants have entered employment, but earnings gaps remain. (Present-perfect contrast)

Migrants have entered employment, yet earnings gaps remain.

10. The family settled after permanent residence had been granted. (Past perfect)

The family settled after permanent residence had been granted.

11. The system lacks coordination, so applicants repeat information. (Nominalisation)

The system’s lack of coordination forces applicants to repeat information.

12. If schools had more resources, children could make up ground faster. (Conditional inversion)

Children could make up ground faster were schools better resourced.

13. Residents opposed the policy because communication was poor. (Cleft cause)

It was poor communication that caused residents to oppose the policy.

14. Integration should create opportunity and preserve equal treatment. (Balanced recommendation)

Integration should both create opportunity and preserve equal treatment.

15. The city introduced the centre gradually, so staff could adapt. (Participle clause)

Introducing the centre gradually, the city gave staff time to adapt.

16. Inspectors changed the rules after workers reported exploitation. (Emphatic do)

Inspectors did change the rules after workers reported exploitation.

17. No factor matters more than access to stable work. (Negative inversion)

Rarely does any factor matter more than access to stable work.

18. The system should be fair, efficient and humane. (Parallelism)

The system should preserve fairness, improve efficiency and protect dignity.

8. Native Academic Toolbox

1. Upgrade: “Migrants cannot use their skills.” using skills underuse.

Skills underuse prevents migrants and host economies from benefiting from existing qualifications.

2. Upgrade: “People live in separate neighbourhoods.” using residential segregation.

Residential segregation can reduce access to schools, jobs and social contact.

3. Upgrade: “The country needs more workers.” using labour shortages.

Persistent labour shortages may justify targeted migration and domestic training.

4. Upgrade: “Qualifications are not accepted.” using credential recognition.

Slow credential recognition pushes experienced professionals into lower-skilled work.

5. Upgrade: “Newcomers need help.” using integration services.

Early integration services can improve language, employment and housing outcomes.

6. Upgrade: “People distrust migration policy.” using public confidence.

Public confidence depends on transparent rules and credible local investment.

7. Upgrade: “Schools are becoming separated.” using school segregation.

School segregation can reproduce inequality across generations.

8. Upgrade: “Migrants earn less.” using earnings gap.

A persistent earnings gap may indicate discrimination or skills mismatch.

9. Upgrade: “The population is getting older.” using demographic ageing.

Demographic ageing increases demand for workers and care services.

10. Upgrade: “Migrants participate in politics.” using civic participation.

Civic participation helps institutions respond to migrant experience.

11. Upgrade: “The city needs joined-up services.” using one-stop centres.

One-stop centres reduce administrative fragmentation for new arrivals.

12. Upgrade: “Employers exploit migrants.” using anti-discrimination law.

Anti-discrimination law and labour inspection should protect migrant workers.

13. Upgrade: “Migration helps the population.” using population renewal.

Migration can support population renewal without eliminating ageing.

14. Upgrade: “People send money home.” using remittances.

Remittances support families and local investment in origin countries.

15. Upgrade: “Policy should involve both sides.” using two-way integration.

Two-way integration requires effort from migrants and responsive institutions.

9. IELTS Speaking

Part 1 · 15 questions

PART 1 · 1

Have you ever lived in another country?

Suggested phrasal verbs
settle infit in
Model answer · 80 words

Yes, I lived abroad for one academic year. The first month was tiring because simple tasks, such as registering with a doctor or understanding a rental contract, required extra attention. I began to settle in once I joined a local sports club and stopped relying only on other international students. I never felt that I had to erase my background to fit in. Instead, learning ordinary routines and asking respectful questions made daily life easier and helped friendships develop naturally.

PART 1 · 2

Would you consider moving abroad for work?

Suggested phrasal verbs
take uppull up stakes
Model answer · 81 words

I would consider it if the position offered real professional development rather than only a higher salary. Before I take up an overseas role, I would compare employment rights, housing costs, healthcare and whether my partner could work. I would also want a clear route for recognising my qualifications. Pulling up stakes can be exciting, but it affects relationships and long-term security. For me, the move would need to improve my career without leaving my family in a permanently uncertain situation.

PART 1 · 3

Is learning the local language important?

Suggested phrasal verbs
make up groundlose ground
Model answer · 79 words

Yes, because even basic language ability makes everyday decisions more independent. It helps people speak to teachers, understand workplace instructions and use public services without always asking someone else to interpret. Children may lose ground if support begins late, while adults can make up ground through flexible classes linked to real situations. Fluency should not be treated as proof of personal worth, but accessible language learning creates more equal contact and gives newcomers a stronger voice in the community.

PART 1 · 4

Do you know people from different countries?

Suggested phrasal verbs
connect withwear away
Model answer · 80 words

Yes, my workplace and neighbourhood are both quite international. I have colleagues from several countries, and our conversations often challenge assumptions that news headlines create. Working on ordinary problems together helps us connect with one another as individuals rather than representatives of a nationality. Over time, that contact can wear away stereotypes because people see differences within every group. These friendships have also made international events feel more concrete, since I understand how policy changes affect actual families and careers.

PART 1 · 5

Would you use a one-stop integration centre?

Suggested phrasal verbs
connect withmiss out
Model answer · 82 words

Yes, especially during the first weeks in a new city. A well-run centre could explain residence documents, schools, healthcare and employment in one visit, so people would be less likely to miss out on an essential service. I would value a named adviser who could connect with specialist agencies when a question became complicated. However, I would check how the centre protects personal data and whether using it is separate from immigration enforcement. Convenience should not come at the cost of privacy.

PART 1 · 6

Should foreign qualifications be recognised faster?

Suggested phrasal verbs
clear the waytake action
Model answer · 82 words

Yes, although faster recognition must still protect professional standards. Authorities should publish deadlines, explain evidence requirements and take action when an application remains unanswered for months. Where genuine gaps exist, a short bridging course is better than forcing someone to repeat an entire degree. This process can clear the way for nurses, engineers or teachers to use their experience. A transparent appeal route would also make the system fairer and reduce the waste caused by skilled people working far below their level.

PART 1 · 7

Do migrants improve city life?

Suggested phrasal verbs
start upgrow steadily
Model answer · 80 words

They often do, although the result depends on local policy. New residents may start up businesses, fill important jobs and add languages, food and cultural knowledge to a city. These benefits can grow steadily when housing, transport and schools expand with the population. If services remain underfunded, however, existing residents may associate newcomers with shortages they did not create. I therefore see migration as a potential strength, provided that city governments plan for change and share its gains across neighbourhoods.

PART 1 · 8

Would you join a community language exchange?

Suggested phrasal verbs
connect withfit in
Model answer · 80 words

Yes, because it would make practice more relaxed than a formal lesson. I could help someone with English while learning their language and hearing about their daily experience. Regular meetings would let us connect with through shared interests instead of treating the exchange as charity. It might also help a newcomer fit in without suggesting that their original identity is a problem. I would prefer a consistent partner or small group, since trust and useful correction usually develop over time.

PART 1 · 9

Is it difficult to settle in a new place?

Suggested phrasal verbs
settle input down roots
Model answer · 83 words

Yes, even when the move is voluntary. People must learn unfamiliar systems while finding housing, managing money and rebuilding a social network. Reliable information can shorten that stressful period, but one friendly neighbour or colleague may matter just as much. It becomes easier to settle in when daily routines stop requiring constant calculation. Putting down roots takes longer because it depends on secure housing, meaningful relationships and confidence that the community will remain a home.

Predictable rules also reduce unnecessary mistakes and embarrassment.

PART 1 · 10

Do you think schools help integration?

Suggested phrasal verbs
make up groundlose ground
Model answer · 81 words

Yes, schools can connect children and parents with the wider community. Pupils learn the local language through real relationships, while families meet teachers and other parents. However, support must begin early so children do not lose ground in subjects while they are learning new vocabulary. Extra language teaching can help them make up ground without lowering academic expectations. Mixed clubs, sports and projects also matter because integration is stronger when children cooperate regularly rather than meeting only at symbolic cultural events.

PART 1 · 11

Would you send money to relatives abroad?

Suggested phrasal verbs
transfer homemiss out
Model answer · 82 words

Yes, if my income allowed it and a relative needed support. I would use a regulated service to transfer home a planned amount each month, rather than responding unpredictably to every request. Remittances can help with school fees, healthcare or urgent repairs, but the sender also has rent and savings goals. I would not want my own household to miss out on essentials. Clear expectations within the family would make the support sustainable and reduce tension about how the money is used.

PART 1 · 12

Should newcomers receive housing support?

Suggested phrasal verbs
put down rootspull up stakes
Model answer · 82 words

They should receive support according to need, using the same clear principles applied to other residents. Temporary help with deposits, information or emergency accommodation can prevent homelessness and allow families to put down roots. However, placing all newcomers in one isolated district may deepen segregation. Governments must also expand affordable supply for the wider population, or long-term residents may pull up stakes as rents rise. Fair housing policy should solve scarcity rather than encourage different groups to blame one another for it.

PART 1 · 13

Is citizenship important for belonging?

Suggested phrasal verbs
settle infit in
Model answer · 82 words

Citizenship is important because it provides secure status, voting rights and formal equality. It can confirm that someone who has settled in is a full member of the political community. However, legal status does not automatically create everyday respect, and people often contribute long before naturalisation. They should not have to abandon language, religion or family traditions merely to fit in. For me, belonging combines enforceable rights with participation, recognition and the ordinary feeling that one's future is connected to a place.

PART 1 · 14

Do international communities change food culture?

Suggested phrasal verbs
start upconnect with
Model answer · 79 words

Yes, and the change is usually creative rather than one culture simply replacing another. Migrants may start up restaurants or shops, but ingredients and recipes also enter home cooking and local markets. Food gives people an easy way to connect with unfamiliar traditions because sharing a meal is concrete and social. Over time, dishes are adapted to local tastes and new combinations become normal. This exchange can create fresh local traditions while older regional food remains available and valued.

PART 1 · 15

Would you volunteer to help new arrivals?

Suggested phrasal verbs
connect withtake action
Model answer · 82 words

Yes, but I would choose a role with practical training and a clear boundary. I could help someone understand transport, practise conversation or complete a form, while a qualified adviser handled legal questions. That would let me take action without pretending to be an expert. I would also want the relationship to be mutual, so we could connect with each other as neighbours rather than helper and recipient. Consistent, modest support is probably more useful than one enthusiastic event followed by silence.

Part 3 · 15 questions

PART 3 · 1

Can migration solve demographic ageing?

Suggested phrasal verbs
take upgrow steadily
Model answer · 118 words

Migration can ease demographic pressure, but it cannot solve ageing by itself. Working-age newcomers may take up vacancies in care, construction and technology, while their taxes support public services. That effect matters quickly, especially in regions losing younger adults. However, migrants also grow older, and rapid population change can strain housing or schools if capacity does not grow steadily. Governments still need productivity investment, childcare, later-life employment and pension reform. Migration is therefore one adaptable component of demographic policy, not a permanent substitute for planning across the whole life course.

It should also avoid recruiting essential staff from countries with worse shortages unless agreements finance training there, protect genuinely voluntary movement and expand local workforce capacity for everyone.

PART 3 · 2

What makes integration successful?

Suggested phrasal verbs
settle input down roots
Model answer · 119 words

Integration succeeds when legal security, language access, fair work and ordinary contact reinforce one another. Newcomers can settle in faster when services are understandable and employers recognise relevant skills, but institutions must also enforce equal treatment. Stable housing allows families to put down roots, while schools and local associations create repeated contact. Success should be measured through job quality, school progress, housing security and participation rather than cultural sameness. The process is two-way: residents need clear expectations and resources, and newcomers need realistic opportunities to meet those expectations without erasing their identities.

Municipalities should publish these outcomes by neighbourhood and length of residence, then revise programmes with migrant organisations instead of treating one national average as proof of success.

PART 3 · 3

Should language courses be compulsory?

Suggested phrasal verbs
make up groundmiss out
Model answer · 119 words

Courses may be compulsory when language ability is genuinely necessary for long-term residence, but obligation is fair only if access is real. Classes should be affordable, nearby and compatible with work or care, so shift workers do not miss out. Learners with disrupted education may need extra time to make up ground, while disability can require an alternative assessment. Practical speaking and listening are more useful than obscure grammar tests. A requirement should therefore encourage participation through support and repeated opportunities, not punish people for barriers that the government itself has failed to remove.

Childcare, transport support and online options can widen access further, while funded community practice gives learners a reason to use the language outside the classroom.

PART 3 · 4

How can governments reduce skills underuse?

Suggested phrasal verbs
clear the waymake up ground
Model answer · 118 words

Governments should begin with faster, transparent credential recognition. Published deadlines, affordable appeals and clear evidence rules can clear the way for qualified residents to enter suitable work. When training systems differ, short bridging courses and supervised practice help candidates make up ground without repeating an entire degree. Employment services should offer sector-specific language support and mentoring, while employers explain rejections consistently. Authorities can then measure progress through job quality and career movement, not simply any placement. This approach protects standards while treating unfamiliar qualifications as evidence to assess rather than a reason to dismiss experience.

Governments should also audit recruitment algorithms and licensing fees, since an efficient recognition office cannot overcome discrimination or costs that exclude low-income applicants.

PART 3 · 5

Does migration lower wages?

Suggested phrasal verbs
take actionlose ground
Model answer · 123 words

The effect varies by sector, bargaining power and the speed at which firms expand. Migration can increase labour supply, yet it also increases demand and may fill shortages that limit production. Wages are most likely to lose ground where employers can exploit insecure status, tie permits to one workplace or avoid collective agreements. Governments should take action through pay inspections, portable work rights and penalties for recruitment debt. Those protections help migrant and established workers together. It is therefore misleading to treat nationality as the cause when weak labour standards often determine who captures the economic gain.

Sector-level data are essential: a small national average may hide serious pressure in one low-wage occupation and simultaneous wage growth in industries where shortages are relieved.

PART 3 · 6

How should cities respond to rapid population growth?

Suggested phrasal verbs
take actionput down roots
Model answer · 120 words

Cities need funding that follows actual population change rather than outdated counts. Councils can then take action by adding classrooms, clinic capacity, public transport and affordable housing before queues become severe. Timely local data should be combined with consultation involving new and established residents. Secure neighbourhood housing helps families put down roots and reduces repeated displacement. National government must share the cost, because municipalities cannot deliver integration through goodwill alone. Visible service expansion also improves public confidence: residents can see that administrative capacity is growing instead of being encouraged to blame newcomers for shortages.

Employers, schools and health providers should share forecasts with councils, allowing capacity to arrive before pressure becomes visible rather than after public confidence has already deteriorated.

PART 3 · 7

What are the benefits of family reunification?

Suggested phrasal verbs
settle input down roots
Model answer · 121 words

Family reunification gives people emotional stability and practical support after migration. Partners can share childcare, income and care for older relatives, making it easier to settle in and plan beyond the next permit renewal. Stable households are also more willing to put down roots, learn the language and participate in schools or neighbourhood organisations. Family members may bring skills and start businesses once they receive prompt work rights. Of course, services need adequate funding, but treating workers as isolated economic units ignores the relationships that help them remain productive, healthy and committed to a community.

Fast processing is equally important because years of uncertainty can postpone employment, housing and education, weakening precisely the stability that family reunion is meant to create.

PART 3 · 8

Should migrants have voting rights?

Suggested phrasal verbs
fit inconnect with
Model answer · 119 words

Long-term residents should normally gain local voting rights after a period of lawful residence, because municipal decisions shape their housing, schools and transport directly. Participation can help people connect with institutions and accept responsibility for shared choices. It also signals that they do not have to demonstrate cultural sameness merely to fit in. National voting may remain linked to citizenship, since it determines the whole constitutional direction of a country. However, advisory forums are not an adequate permanent substitute for a genuine vote where residents pay taxes and are affected by local policy.

Eligibility should be clear and reviewed regularly, while language access and civic education help every voter understand local institutions and evaluate competing claims independently and fairly.

PART 3 · 9

How can schools prevent segregation?

Suggested phrasal verbs
make up groundlose ground
Model answer · 118 words

Admissions policy should avoid concentrating disadvantage in a small number of schools, and funding should follow pupils' actual needs. Trained language staff can help newcomers make up ground without lowering expectations in mathematics, science or literature. If support arrives late, children may lose ground academically while also becoming socially isolated. Mixed extracurricular activities, shared projects and parent participation create sustained contact across backgrounds. Schools should monitor outcomes by language, income and time since arrival, then intervene early. The objective is not to erase difference but to ensure that difference does not predict educational opportunity.

Authorities should also monitor whether informal selection, transport boundaries or housing patterns quietly recreate separation even when official admissions rules appear formally neutral today.

PART 3 · 10

Why does public opinion become polarised?

Suggested phrasal verbs
wear awayconnect with
Model answer · 118 words

Polarisation grows when real local pressure receives an exaggerated or selective explanation. Residents may face scarce housing, while political actors present newcomers as the sole cause and confuse annual flows with the total migrant population. Limited contact makes that story easier to believe. Equal-status projects can wear away stereotypes, and transparent consultation helps people connect with policy decisions rather than rumours. Governments must also fund services and enforce rules against exploitation. Trust will not return through optimistic slogans alone; people need accurate data, visible delivery and confidence that rights and responsibilities apply consistently.

Journalists and public bodies can help by distinguishing workers, students, refugees and family members instead of presenting migration as one undifferentiated movement with one effect.

PART 3 · 11

Can temporary migration benefit both countries?

Suggested phrasal verbs
take uptransfer home
Model answer · 118 words

Yes, when contracts are fair and mobility is genuinely voluntary. Workers can take up shortage roles, develop skills and transfer home income that supports education or investment. Employers gain labour, while origin countries may gain professional networks when people return. However, benefits disappear if recruitment debt, tied permits or withheld passports create dependence. Programmes therefore need portable social benefits, freedom to change employers and recognition of experience after return. Temporary status should describe the planned duration, not justify weaker rights. Without those safeguards, circular migration becomes a convenient label for permanent vulnerability.

Origin and destination governments should share enforcement data and provide accessible complaints, since a contract signed abroad is useless when the worker cannot challenge abuse effectively.

PART 3 · 12

How should asylum systems balance speed and fairness?

Suggested phrasal verbs
take actionclear the way
Model answer · 118 words

Speed and fairness support each other when systems are properly staffed. Governments should take action by hiring trained decision-makers, providing early legal advice and setting realistic appeal deadlines. Rapid triage can clear the way for urgent or straightforward cases, but numerical targets must not replace individual evidence. Applicants need interpreters and humane reception conditions so they can present accurate testimony. Independent review should identify repeated errors, while authorities publish waiting times honestly. A fast wrong decision merely transfers delay to appeals or courts; a fair initial process gives both applicants and the public a reliable answer.

Decision-makers also need current country information and trauma-aware interviewing, because apparent inconsistency may reflect fear, translation problems or memory affected by violence.

PART 3 · 13

What role do employers play in integration?

Suggested phrasal verbs
start uptake up
Model answer · 118 words

Employers shape access to income, colleagues and professional recognition, so their role is substantial. They should assess overseas qualifications consistently, explain rejections and offer paid language or skills training. Newcomers who take up suitable work can rebuild confidence and social networks, while those who start up businesses may create jobs for others. Employers must also respond to harassment and avoid using permit dependence as leverage. Regulators still matter, because voluntary good practice is uneven. Fair recruitment helps integration when workplace rights are enforceable and career progression remains possible after the first job.

Small firms need guidance as well as enforcement, since they may want to recruit fairly but lack expertise in overseas credentials, language support or immigration rules.

PART 3 · 14

Can migration prevent population decline?

Suggested phrasal verbs
put down rootspull up stakes
Model answer · 118 words

Migration can slow decline, particularly in regions losing young adults and essential services. New residents may support schools, fill vacancies and create businesses, but they will put down roots only if housing, transport and suitable work are available. Otherwise they may pull up stakes for a larger city or another country. Migration also cannot reverse low birth rates indefinitely because migrants age too. Regional investment, family policy and productivity growth therefore remain necessary. The realistic aim is to manage decline and renewal together, not recruit people as permanent demographic replacements.

Local participation is crucial: people are more likely to stay when they can influence services, gain secure status and see a stable and credible future for their children.

PART 3 · 15

What would a fair migration system look like?

Suggested phrasal verbs
take actiongrow steadily
Model answer · 122 words

A fair system would offer regular pathways for work, family and protection, with decisions made promptly and explained clearly. Rights would be enforceable, responsibilities realistic and local services funded according to need. Authorities should take action against exploitation by employers, recruiters or smugglers rather than treating vulnerability as wrongdoing. Independent oversight would test consistency across nationality, income and legal category. Integration capacity must grow steadily with population change through housing, language access and schools. Fairness does not mean every applicant receives permission; it means relevant evidence is heard and the same transparent rules govern comparable cases.

Regular public reporting should show who waits, who succeeds on appeal and whether local capacity keeps pace, so fairness can be tested rather than merely promised.

10. Five IELTS Writing Task 2 topics

Before writing: check that each body paragraph has a clear topic sentence, explanation, development and a relevant consequence or example. Your position must remain consistent from the introduction to the conclusion.
TASK 2 · 1

Migrants should be required to pass a language test before receiving permanent residence. To what extent do you agree or disagree?

Optional collocation bank
permanent residencelanguage traininglanguage acquisitionmiss outlabour-market inclusiondelayed-integration costexploitation-risk appraisalevidence thresholdequal treatment
Model essay · 302 words

A shared language can support independence and participation, so governments are entitled to encourage serious study before granting permanent residence. However, I disagree with making one high-stakes test an absolute gate. A fair system should combine a reasonable standard with accessible language training and exceptions for people whose circumstances make formal testing unusually difficult.

Supporters of testing make a strong practical case. Everyday communication helps residents use transport, speak to teachers and understand workplace rules. A transparent requirement may motivate early language acquisition, while employers and public agencies benefit when fewer interactions require interpretation. In this sense, a test can signal that settlement involves responsibilities as well as rights.

Nevertheless, test performance is not the same as willingness to integrate. Refugees who experienced interrupted schooling, older applicants and people with disabilities may need more time or a different assessment. Shift workers may also miss out on classes that run during office hours. Denying secure status can then deepen anxiety and delay labour-market inclusion, producing a delayed-integration cost rather than better communication.

The strongest policy would provide affordable courses, childcare, evening schedules and repeated attempts. Assessment should include practical speaking and listening, not obscure grammar. Authorities should recognise documented effort and use an exploitation-risk appraisal before tying a worker's status to an employer. These safeguards preserve an evidence threshold and ensure equal treatment without abandoning the expectation of participation.

In conclusion, language ability is a legitimate integration goal but an inflexible pass-or-leave rule is disproportionate. Governments should set a realistic standard, fund the route towards it and allow carefully defined alternatives. Such a system protects public confidence while treating permanent residence as the outcome of supported participation, not success in a single examination. This balance is more likely to produce genuine communication than a punitive barrier that leaves motivated residents in lasting insecurity.

TASK 2 · 2

Some people believe family reunification supports integration, while others think migration should be based mainly on labour-market needs. Discuss both views and give your opinion.

Optional collocation bank
family reunificationjob vacancieslabour migrationcredential recognitionsettle input down rootsfair-recruitment frameworkintegration servicesshared civic benefit
Model essay · 302 words

Debate about migration often contrasts economic selection with family life. Labour-based routes can fill shortages and make the initial purpose of admission clear, whereas family reunification can stabilise households after arrival. In my view, labour demand should shape part of the system, but it should not override the right to live with close family members.

The labour-market argument is understandable. Employers may face persistent job vacancies in health, construction or care, and carefully designed labour migration can match people to those needs. When credential recognition is quick and recruitment is fair, new workers contribute taxes, expand services and reduce immediate pressure on public budgets. Transparent criteria may also strengthen trust in the admission process.

Yet workers do not live as isolated units of production. Long separation can cause stress, weaken childcare arrangements and make settlement temporary. Partners and adult relatives often bring skills, provide emotional support and help children settle in. Stable households are more able to put down roots, learn the local language and participate in schools and neighbourhood organisations.

Governments should therefore maintain both routes and connect them to integration support. Labour admissions need a fair-recruitment framework so that employers cannot exploit permit dependence. Family members should receive prompt work rights, language classes and access to integration services. Investment in housing and schools should follow actual local population change, ensuring that receiving areas share a shared civic benefit rather than an unfunded burden.

In conclusion, labour-market needs justify targeted migration pathways, but they are not a complete moral or economic framework. Family reunification supports durable settlement and recognises that human beings have relationships beyond employment. A balanced system should recruit needed workers, protect family unity and give every admitted resident a credible route into work and civic life. Economic planning works better when it recognises the household behind the worker.

TASK 2 · 3

Cities are creating one-stop centres for migrants. Do the advantages outweigh the disadvantages?

Optional collocation bank
one-stop centresresidence permitspublic schoolsmiss outmunicipal integration strategyintegration outcome indicatorsdata minimisationinternational migrationmigration flows
Model essay · 300 words

New arrivals often have to contact separate offices for legal status, housing, schools, healthcare and employment. One-stop centres bring these services together in a single accessible location. Although centralisation can create privacy and capacity risks, I believe the advantages outweigh the disadvantages when agencies retain specialist expertise and clear accountability.

The main benefit is practical coordination. A resident who asks about a residence permit may also need a language course or help enrolling a child in public schools. Co-located staff can identify connected needs, reduce contradictory instructions and refer urgent cases quickly. This model lowers administrative friction for newcomers and prevents expensive duplication across departments.

Centres can also improve policy evidence. With consent and proper safeguards, staff can see where delays repeatedly occur and whether people miss out on a service. Municipalities can use these patterns to refine a municipal integration strategy and set meaningful integration outcome indicators. Face-to-face help remains particularly valuable for residents who lack digital confidence or stable internet access.

The disadvantages are real. A large centre may become a bottleneck, and careless data sharing can expose sensitive information. Some people may avoid a visibly migrant-labelled building because they fear stigma or enforcement. Cities should therefore apply data minimisation, provide independent complaints, maintain neighbourhood outreach and separate service access from immigration control unless the law clearly requires otherwise.

In conclusion, integrated centres solve a genuine institutional problem: fragmented services transfer coordination costs to the people least able to bear them. Their benefits outweigh the risks if cities fund enough staff, protect confidentiality and preserve alternative access routes. Properly designed, a one-stop centre does not reduce migrants to one category; it helps several public institutions respond to a whole person. Regular independent review should confirm in practice that convenience has not displaced specialist judgment, privacy or meaningful choice.

TASK 2 · 4

Many highly qualified migrants work in jobs below their skill level. What problems does this cause, and what solutions should governments introduce?

Optional collocation bank
skills underuseearnings gapmental wellbeingentry-level rolesnewcomer-inclusive growthfiscal contributioncredential recognitionskills matchingprovide paid training
Model essay · 301 words

When qualified migrants work far below their level, the problem is usually described as skills underuse. It harms individuals through lower income and stalled careers, but it also wastes scarce expertise in the wider economy. Governments should address both the recognition of overseas qualifications and the practical barriers that prevent employers from using them.

For individuals, persistent mismatch creates an earnings gap and weakens professional identity. A nurse driving a taxi may lose recent clinical experience, while a trained engineer may struggle to provide evidence of past projects. Families have less capacity to invest in housing and education, and frustration can damage mental wellbeing even when the person is technically employed.

Society also bears costs. Hospitals, schools and firms may report shortages while qualified residents remain in unrelated entry-level roles. Tax revenue is lower, public training already completed abroad is wasted, and stereotypes can harden when employers repeatedly overlook unfamiliar credentials. This slows newcomer-inclusive growth and reduces the visible fiscal contribution of migration.

The first solution is faster, transparent credential recognition with published time limits and affordable appeals. Where genuine gaps exist, governments should fund short bridging courses, supervised practice and occupation-specific language support. Public employment services can improve skills matching, while employers receiving recruitment subsidies should provide paid training and explain rejections consistently.

In conclusion, occupational mismatch is neither an inevitable first step nor a private disappointment. It is a correctable failure to translate experience across institutions. By improving recognition, targeted training and fair recruitment, governments can help skilled residents resume productive careers, relieve shortages and demonstrate that integration rewards competence rather than familiarity with one country's paperwork. Progress should be measured through job quality and career movement, not simply the speed of any placement. That distinction prevents governments from celebrating employment while valuable professional ability continues to be wasted.

TASK 2 · 5

Why do some communities oppose immigration? How can governments build public confidence while protecting migrants’ rights?

Optional collocation bank
equal treatmentresident sentimentmigration flowsmigrant stockmigration-policy accountabilityinclusive policy dialogueanti-discrimination lawwear awaylocal displacement
Model essay · 301 words

Opposition to immigration rarely has one cause. It may reflect cultural anxiety, rapid local change, competition for scarce housing or distrust of national authorities. Some concerns are based on real service pressure, while others are amplified by misinformation. Governments can build confidence only by addressing material problems honestly while defending equal treatment and the rule of law.

Housing and public services are central. When population grows in a municipality with too few homes, classrooms or doctors, residents may experience longer waits and rising rents. If national leaders celebrate aggregate economic gains but leave councils underfunded, resident sentiment deteriorates. Newcomers then become a visible target for shortages that often began years earlier.

Information also shapes attitudes. Sensational coverage can confuse annual migration flows with the total migrant stock or treat refugees, students and workers as one group. Limited everyday contact allows stereotypes to persist. Political actors may exploit that uncertainty, making people feel that neither borders nor public spending face migration-policy accountability.

Governments should publish clear local data, fund services according to actual demand and use inclusive policy dialogue that includes newcomers and established residents. Strong anti-discrimination law must be enforced, not traded away for popularity. At the same time, visible action against labour exploitation and unsafe housing shows that rules apply to employers and landlords as well as migrants. Community projects can wear away prejudice through equal-status cooperation.

In conclusion, confidence does not require authorities to validate hostility or weaken rights. It requires competent delivery, honest explanation and fair participation. When institutions prevent local displacement, respond to pressure and protect every resident from abuse, immigration becomes a manageable civic issue rather than a symbol of government losing control. Public trust becomes durable over the long term when residents can see problems being solved without seeing vulnerable people used as the solution.

Course index · Module 1 · Chapter 17

Academic English Studio · Topic 17 · Migration, Integration and Belonging
